title: Una Chula II
creator: Juan Luna
description: Una Chula II is a 1885 painting portraying a lower class Madrileña (woman from Madrid) sitting squarely with arms resting on a chair, a pose that “almost mannishly exuding sexual confidence and worldliness, holding a lit cigarette between two fingers in a flirtatious way. The burning tip of the cigarette acts as an enhancement to the female’s “painted lips” that supports a “slight smile”.

title: The Parisian Life
creator: Juan Luna
description: The Parisian Life, also known as Interior d'un Cafe, literally meaning "Inside a Café", is a 1892 oil on canvas painting. It portrayed a scene inside a café in Paris with a woman identified as a courtesan or a prostitute representing "fallen womanhood", who was about to rise from a sofa overshadowing three men placed at the far left corner of the painting. Apart from the prominent figure of the female wearing a pale lavender frock and a hat embellished with flowers, fronted by two glasses of beers and an empty beer mug belying a "company of men", The Parisian Life portrayed a glimpse of Luna's own life in the capital of France while accompanied by two close friends.

title: Governor Ramon Blanco
creator: Juan Luna
description: A formal portrait of Governor-General Ramón Blanco, capturing the stoic authority of one of the final Spanish rulers of the Philippines. Painted during the 1880s, this work is a key example of Luna’s role as an official portraitist for the colonial elite, blending academic precision with a sharp psychological presence.

title: Spoliarium
creator: Juan Luna
description: Spoliarium depicts bloody carcasses of slave gladiators being dragged away from the arena where they had entertained their Roman masters, with their lives. The painting was submitted by Luna to the Exposición Nacional de Bellas Artes in 1884 in Madrid, where it garnered the first gold medal. In 1886, it was sold to the Diputación Provincial de Barcelona for 20,000 pesetas. It currently hangs in the main gallery at the ground floor of the National Museum of the Philippines, and is the first work of art that greets visitors upon entry into the museum.

title: El Pacto de Sangre
creator: Juan Luna
description: A monumental depiction of the 1565 peace treaty between the Spanish explorer Miguel López de Legazpi and Datu Sikatuna of Bohol. This 1885 masterpiece symbolizes the 'Treaty of Friendship' between Spain and the Philippines, though Filipino patriots later reinterpreted it as a broken promise of equality and brotherhood

title: Despues del Baile
creator: Juan Luna
description: A quiet study of exhaustion and elegance, Después del Baile captures a moment of fatigue following a grand social event. Luna masterfully utilizes a muted palette and soft, loose brushwork to depict a woman in repose, highlighting the contrast between her lavish attire and the heavy, somber mood of the morning after
subject: The figure is often seen as a symbol of the fin de siècle (end of the century) sentiment—a mix of luxury, boredom, and quiet melancholy

title: Puesta Del Sol
creator: Juan Luna
description: A masterclass in light and color, Puesta del Sol captures the fleeting, golden transition of the day into night. Luna moves away from figurative drama to focus on the raw beauty of nature, using vibrant oranges and deep violets to evoke the serene and melancholic atmosphere of a seaside sunset
subject: It highlights Luna's versatility; he wasn't just a painter of people and politics, but also a sensitive observer of the natural world

title: Idilio
creator: Juan Luna
description: A poetic transition from darkness to light, Idilio depicts an idealized female figure in a state of romantic reverie. Set in a fairytale landscape where sunset and daylight coexist, Luna utilizes a rich, golden palette and thick impasto to convey a moment of timeless happiness, blending 19th-century fashion with the timeless themes of Greek and Roman mythology
subject: The figure is often interpreted as Iris, the Greek goddess of the rainbow and a protectress of the sky, identified by the irises surrounding her. She wears a golden robe and a 19th-century draped corset, merging contemporary style with myth.

title: La Muerte de Cleopatra
creator: Juan Luna
description: A chillingly beautiful depiction of the final moments of the Queen of Egypt. Luna captures the immediate aftermath of Cleopatra’s suicide, emphasizing the heavy atmosphere of a fallen dynasty. The work is a masterclass in the 'Grand Style' of academic history painting, blending archaeological detail with high theatrical drama.

title: Mi Hermana
creator: Juan Luna
description: An exquisite study in portraiture, this work demonstrates Luna’s ability to manipulate light to create psychological depth. The soft modeling of the face against a dark, nondescript background allows the subject’s expression to take center stage, showcasing a restrained yet powerful use of the academic style.
subject: The painting is believed to depict one of Juan Luna's sisters, likely Numeriana or Remedios.

title: Esopo
creator: Juan Luna
description: Esopo is a fascinating character study from Juan Luna’s time in Europe. It represents his engagement with the "philosopher portraits" that were popular among European academic painters, inspired by the works of Spanish masters like Diego Velázquez and Jusepe de Ribera.
subject: Aesop was an ancient Greek storyteller credited with a number of fables (like The Tortoise and the Hare). Historically, he was said to have been a slave who gained his freedom through his wit.

title: The Battle of Lepanto
creator: Juan Luna
description: A monumental commemoration of the 1571 naval victory of the Holy League over the Ottoman Empire. Commissioned by the Spanish Senate, this work was intended to celebrate Spanish naval power and the defense of Christendom, famously featuring Don Juan of Austria in the heat of command. It remains a rare instance of a Filipino artist defining Spain's national historical narrative.
subject: The central figure is Don Juan of Austria, the illegitimate son of Emperor Charles V, shown on the prow of his galley, the Real, leading the charge.

title: Indio Bravo
creator: Juan Luna
description: Often characterized by its raw, sketch-like quality, Luna’s work in this vein focuses on the physical and psychological strength of the Filipino man. Utilizing bold, confident strokes and a focus on anatomy, he creates a portrait of a 'new' Filipino—one that is physically robust and intellectually enlightened.
